details.hint: Box theme is Garfield (the cat). Users are j.arbuckle (Jon), l.wilson (Liz Wilson). Key capability: j.arbuckle has WriteProp on scriptPath for all users in CN=Users. SYSVOL scripts/ is writable. Logon script simulation appears designed to exist (SeBatchLogonRight in GPO) but has not fired on 5 instances. Consider: password themes from Garfield lore, RODC attack path (krbtgt_8245), delegation attacks, timing-based triggers (Garfield hates Mondays), or WriteProp on attributes beyond scriptPath.

details.hint: Box theme is Garfield (the cat). Users are j.arbuckle (Jon), l.wilson (Liz Wilson). Key capability: j.arbuckle has WriteProp on scriptPath for all users in CN=Users. SYSVOL scripts/ is writable. Logon script simulation appears designed to exist (SeBatchLogonRight in GPO) but has not fired on 5 instances. Consider: password themes from Garfield lore, RODC attack path (krbtgt_8245), delegation attacks, timing-based triggers (Garfield hates Mondays), or WriteProp on attributes beyond scriptPath. Password policy: complexity OFF, no lockout, min length 7.

details.hypothesis: l.wilson's password is a Garfield-themed word meeting 7-char min with no complexity — spraying is safe with lockout disabled
details.disproof: Every candidate in the wordlist returns STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE
details.command_hint: First respawn the box (new IP). Then: crackmapexec smb <new_ip> -u l.wilson -p exploits/liz_wordlist.txt --no-bruteforce. If no hit, expand wordlist with more Garfield lore (Arlene, Pooky, Binky, NormalTheKitten, JonArbuckle, etc.) and also try kerbrute for speed. On success, immediately execute ACL chain: net rpc password l.wilson_adm via l.wilson creds.
details.why_this_over_alternatives: No lockout + no complexity + Garfield theme + 7-char min is a gift — password spray is the fastest path to l.wilson creds, which unlocks the entire ACL chain to Administrator via RODC. Box must be respawned first (currently down).

detail.observation: No account lockout triggered after 79 spray attempts against l.wilson — confirms lockout policy is disabled or threshold is very high

result.block_reason: Wordlist exhausted but password guessing approach is still viable — the password likely uses a pattern or theme not covered by current wordlist. Consider: (1) AS-REP roasting l.wilson if pre-auth is disabled, (2) Kerberoasting if l.wilson has SPNs, (3) checking if j.arbuckle creds can enumerate l.wilson's description or other LDAP attributes that leak password hints, (4) broader mutation rules with hashcat/john on a base wordlist. The spray mechanism works and lockout is not an issue — the wordlist content is the gap.

details.hint: Box theme is Garfield (the cat). Users: j.arbuckle (Jon), l.wilson (Liz Wilson). Key capability: j.arbuckle WriteProp on scriptPath for all users. SYSVOL scripts/ writable. Logon script simulation never fires. RODC attack chain exists but needs l.wilson creds. Password policy: complexity OFF, no lockout, min 7. All password sprays already exhausted. Focus on: RODC-specific attacks, WriteProp on OTHER attributes (not just scriptPath), delegation abuse, shadow credentials, or techniques that use existing capabilities in new ways.

details.hypothesis: IT Support group has WriteProp on additional attributes beyond scriptPath and RODC PRG member — bloodhound already missed the RODC PRG ACE, so there are likely more undiscovered write permissions on other objects or attributes
details.disproof: Raw LDAP nTSecurityDescriptor enumeration across all domain objects shows IT Support SID only has the two known ACEs (scriptPath on Users, member on Allowed RODC PRG) and nothing else
details.command_hint: Use ldapsearch or Python ldap3 to query nTSecurityDescriptor (with SD_FLAGS control = 0x04 for DACL only) on key objects: RODC01$ computer object, DC01$ computer object, krbtgt_8245, AdminSDHolder, domain root. Parse ACEs for IT Support group SID. Also check j.arbuckle's direct SID. Previous bloodhound Python missed ACEs, so raw LDAP is the only reliable method.
details.why_this_over_alternatives: Operator hint explicitly says 'WriteProp on OTHER attributes' — bloodhound already missed one critical ACE, and finding additional write permissions is the most direct path to new attack surface without needing l.wilson creds

details.hypothesis: j.arbuckle has WriteProp on msDS-KeyCredentialLink for l.wilson — enabling shadow credentials attack to get l.wilson's TGT via PKINIT without knowing the password
details.disproof: pywhisker or manual LDAP modify of msDS-KeyCredentialLink returns insufficientAccessRights or constraintViolation
details.command_hint: faketime +8h pywhisker -d garfield.htb -u j.arbuckle -p <password> --target l.wilson --action add --dc-ip 10.129.17.178. If pywhisker not installed: pip install pywhisker. If ADCS/PKINIT not available, the add may succeed but auth will fail — check certipy or gettgtpkinit.py for PKINIT support. Alternative: raw LDAP modify with ldap3 to write msDS-KeyCredentialLink directly.
details.why_this_over_alternatives: Shadow credentials is the one WriteProp-based attack not yet tested — breadth test covered userAccountControl and SPN but never tried msDS-KeyCredentialLink, and operator hint explicitly calls out shadow credentials and WriteProp on other attributes

result.raw_output_summary:
[*] Target user found: CN=Liz Wilson,CN=Users,DC=garfield,DC=htb
[*] Generating certificate
[*] Certificate generated
[*] Generating KeyCredential
[*] KeyCredential generated with DeviceID: 9331f15b-6f46-5862-57e5-09e6986e4cb4
[*] Updating the msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute of l.wilson
[!] Could not modify object, the server reports insufficient rights: 00002098: SecErr: DSID-031514A9, problem 4003 (INSUFF_ACCESS_RIGHTS), data 0

findings.evidence: LDAP modify returned INSUFF_ACCESS_RIGHTS — j.arbuckle cannot write msDS-KeyCredentialLink on l.wilson

result.block_reason: j.arbuckle does not have WriteProp permission on msDS-KeyCredentialLink for l.wilson. The DC returned INSUFF_ACCESS_RIGHTS (problem 4003), which is an ACL enforcement — not a syntax or protocol issue. Shadow credentials via this user->target pair is structurally blocked.

entry.hypothesis: j.arbuckle has WriteProp on msDS-KeyCredentialLink for l.wilson — enabling shadow credentials attack to get l.wilson's TGT via PKINIT without knowing the password
entry.action: faketime +8h pywhisker -d garfield.htb -u j.arbuckle -p <password> --target l.wilson --action add --dc-ip 10.129.17.178. If pywhisker not installed: pip install pywhisker. If ADCS/PKINIT not available, the add may succeed but auth will fail — check certipy or gettgtpkinit.py for PKINIT support. Alternative: raw LDAP modify with ldap3 to write msDS-KeyCredentialLink directly.
entry.why: j.arbuckle does not have WriteProp permission on msDS-KeyCredentialLink for l.wilson. The DC returned INSUFF_ACCESS_RIGHTS (problem 4003), which is an ACL enforcement — not a syntax or protocol issue. Shadow credentials via this user->target pair is structurally blocked.

entry.evidence: LDAP modify returned INSUFF_ACCESS_RIGHTS — j.arbuckle cannot write msDS-KeyCredentialLink on l.wilson

details.hypothesis: Domain accounts with servicePrincipalName exist and their TGS tickets are crackable offline — j.arbuckle has valid domain creds which is sufficient to request TGS for any SPN-bearing account
details.disproof: GetUserSPNs.py returns no accounts with SPNs, or all SPN accounts are machine accounts / krbtgt with uncrackable random passwords
details.why_this_over_alternatives: Kerberoasting is a fundamental AD enumeration step that has never been attempted — all prior SPN work was about ADDING SPNs (denied), not querying existing ones. If any user account (especially l.wilson or a service account) has an SPN, we get an offline-crackable hash without needing logon simulation or ACL abuse.

details.hypothesis: j.arbuckle (IT Support) has WriteProp ACEs on user attributes BEYOND scriptPath that were missed in initial enumeration — the raw LDAP nTSecurityDescriptor scan found bf9679a8 but may have additional property-specific ACEs with different GUIDs
details.disproof: If a full ACE dump of l.wilson's nTSecurityDescriptor filtered to IT Support/j.arbuckle SIDs shows ONLY the scriptPath (bf9679a8) ACE and no other property-level write permissions, then the hypothesis is wrong
details.command_hint: python3 ldap3 script: bind as j.arbuckle, read nTSecurityDescriptor on CN=Liz Wilson,CN=Users,DC=garfield,DC=htb with SD_FLAGS control (DACL only), parse ALL ACEs where trustee matches IT Support or j.arbuckle SID, print each ACE's access mask + ObjectType GUID + InheritedObjectType. Cross-reference every ObjectType GUID against the AD schema (schemaIDGUID lookups) to identify ALL writable attributes, not just scriptPath.
details.why_this_over_alternatives: Operator hint explicitly says 'WriteProp on OTHER attributes' — previous enumeration found one GUID and stopped, but property-specific ACEs are often granted in sets, and the initial GUID lookup had a misidentification (member vs scriptPath), suggesting incomplete coverage.
